content.text: are secondary language models that an agent system switches to when the primary model is unavailable, too slow, or too expensive for a given request. Rather than surfacing an error, the agent transparently retries with a less preferred model—trading some capability for continuity of service.

attrs.alt: Fallback model chain: Router tries Primary (claude-opus-4), fails over to Fallback 1 (claude-sonnet-4-5), Fallback 2 (claude-haiku-4), and Emergency (local model). Each level produces a progressively degraded but valid response.

content.text: A fallback model is any model that a system uses
only when a preferred model cannot serve a request

. The preferred model—usually called the primary—is the one that best satisfies cost, latency, and quality requirements under normal conditions. When the primary fails (rate limit, timeout, API error, or budget exhaustion), the system automatically retries with the next model in a predefined priority list.

In agentic systems the concept extends naturally: an orchestrating agent may call different LLM endpoints for planning, tool-calling, and summarisation. Each call-site can have its own fallback chain, so a single agent workflow might use claude-opus-4 for complex reasoning, fall back to claude-sonnet-4-5 when the primary is rate-limited, and fall back further to claude-haiku-4 for low-complexity steps.

primary model (first preference), fallback (next preference), fallback chain (ordered list), circuit breaker (component that skips a model temporarily after N failures), and graceful degradation (maintaining partial functionality instead of total failure).

Agentic workflows are long-running and involve multi-step reasoning. A single-model setup creates a hard dependency: if that model's API goes down, the entire agent stops. In production, any of the following can interrupt a model API:
per-minute or per-day token quotas imposed by the provider.
HTTP 500, 503, and gateway timeouts that may last seconds to minutes.
planned maintenance or unplanned incidents that last hours.
spending limits reached mid-month in usage-based billing.
a payload that fits one model's window but not a smaller fallback's.

from any single model's availability. They are especially important for customer-facing agents where silent failures damage trust, and for batch processing pipelines where re-running hours of work is expensive.
the system stays operational even when the primary model's API is degraded.
cheap fast models can serve simple sub-tasks, reserving expensive capacity for complex steps. A fallback chain can also be used proactively by routing cheap tasks to the cheapest capable model.
if the primary is slow, a faster fallback can cut end-to-end latency. Some systems fire both models in parallel and use whichever replies first.
users get a reduced-quality answer rather than an error page. For many applications a 70% quality answer delivered instantly beats a 100% quality answer 10 minutes later.
routing across providers reduces dependency on a single vendor's SLA.
a response from the fallback may differ significantly from what the primary would have produced. In multi-turn agents this can cause confusion if the agent's reasoning style changes mid-conversation.
fallback models may have smaller context windows. If the primary accepts 200k tokens, a fallback accepting 8k may not fit the full conversation history.
if rate-limiting frequently triggers fallback to a model priced higher per token, costs can increase unexpectedly.
a misconfigured fallback can silently swallow errors that should surface, making debugging harder. Log every fallback event.
naive retry-then-fallback adds one round-trip delay per attempt. Aggressive timeouts and parallel hedging can mitigate this.
not all models support the same tool/function-calling schemas. Falling back to a model that lacks structured output support can break downstream parsing.

combined with a retry policy and (optionally) a circuit breaker. When a call fails, the system increments a failure counter for that model, selects the next model in the list, and retries. The circuit breaker short-circuits the slow retry path by temporarily skipping a model after it has failed N times within a window, preventing the agent from wasting time on a model that is clearly down.

The evaluation logic at each step is: did the model return a valid response within the timeout budget? A valid response is one that (a) arrived before the deadline, (b) did not return a 4xx/5xx error, and (c) parsed correctly (e.g., valid JSON if the agent expects structured output). All three conditions must hold.
The diagram above shows the standard fallback chain. A
sits in front of all model calls. It maintains a live failure count per model and applies a circuit breaker with three states:
(skip this model). When the circuit for the primary is OPEN, the router immediately advances to Fallback 1 without attempting the primary.

Each successful call exits to the right. Each failed call falls through to the next tier. The emergency fallback at the bottom is typically a self-hosted open-source model (e.g., Llama 3 or Mistral) that can always respond, even during a full outage of cloud providers, though at significantly reduced capability.
users should never see a raw API error. Any fallback is better than a 500.
multi-hour pipelines cannot afford to fail and restart just because one model is temporarily rate-limited.
use the fallback chain proactively. Route simple classification or extraction tasks to haiku-tier models by design, and only escalate to opus-tier when complexity warrants it.
if you use models from multiple providers, one provider's outage should not take down the whole system.
configure fallback to fire when cost-per-token thresholds are exceeded, not just on errors.

if your task has strict output-format or capability requirements that only one specific model can meet, falling back to a weaker model produces results that fail downstream validation anyway. Surface the error explicitly instead of returning a fallback response that will fail silently later.
Define the model priority list: order models from most preferred (highest quality) to least preferred (most available/cheapest). Store this list in config, not code.
Set per-model timeout budgets: the primary gets the longest timeout; fallbacks get progressively shorter budgets because they should be faster.
Instrument a circuit breaker: track failures per model in a shared counter (Redis or in-memory). Open the circuit after N failures in T seconds; close it again after a cool-down.
Wrap all model calls in a retry loop that advances the model index on failure.
Log every fallback event with the model name, error type, and latency. This data drives future tuning of thresholds and model order.
Test each fallback path in staging: kill the primary endpoint and verify that agents degrade gracefully rather than crashing.
Handle context-window differences: if a fallback has a smaller context window, implement a context-truncation strategy (drop oldest messages or summarise history) before re-sending.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Priority-ordered: most capable → cheapest / most available
MODEL_CHAIN = [
    "claude-opus-4-5",
    "claude-sonnet-4-5",
    "claude-haiku-4-5",
]

def call_with_fallback(messages: list[dict], max_tokens: int = 1024) -> str:
    """
    Try each model in order. Return the first successful response.
    Raise RuntimeError only if every model fails.
    """
    last_error = None
    for model in MODEL_CHAIN:
        try:
            response = client.messages.create(
                model=model,
                max_tokens=max_tokens,
                messages=messages,
            )
            if model != MODEL_CHAIN[0]:
                print(f"[fallback] Used {model} instead of {MODEL_CHAIN[0]}")
            return response.content[0].text
        except anthropic.RateLimitError as e:
            last_error = e
            print(f"[fallback] {model} rate-limited, trying next")
        except anthropic.APIStatusError as e:
            last_error = e
            print(f"[fallback] {model} returned {e.status_code}, trying next")
        except Exception as e:
            last_error = e
            print(f"[fallback] {model} failed ({type(e).__name__}), trying next")
    raise RuntimeError(f"All models in chain failed. Last error: {last_error}")

result = call_with_fallback([
    {"role": "user", "content": "Summarise quantum entanglement in two sentences."}
])
print(result)
Circuit breaker fallback with per-model state tracking

A research agent scans 50 URLs, extracts key facts, and synthesises a report. The job runs overnight as a batch. Under normal conditions it uses claude-opus-4-5 for synthesis and claude-haiku-4-5 for per-URL extraction. At 2 AM the Anthropic API hits a transient incident affecting Opus. Without fallbacks the entire job crashes at URL 23. With fallbacks, the circuit breaker detects 5 consecutive failures and opens the Opus circuit; the synthesis step falls back to claude-sonnet-4-5 for the remainder of the run, completing the job with slightly shorter summaries. The team sees a log entry noting the fallback; the report arrives on schedule.
Retrying the same model repeatedly before falling back:
adding 3 retries per model before advancing to the next means the user waits 3x the timeout. Keep retries at 1–2 per model before advancing.
fallback is invisible by design, which makes it invisible to operators. Without logs, you cannot tell if your primary model has been unavailable for days.
Assuming same context window for all models:
sending a 100k-token prompt to a fallback with a 16k limit produces an immediate error, not a fallback success.
Falling back silently on validation errors:
if the primary returns a malformed tool call, the problem may be in your prompt. Blindly falling back hides prompt bugs.
without one, every call to a down model wastes the full timeout period before advancing. In a 50-step agent that is 50 times the full timeout in dead time.
a tool defined for one model may not work identically on a non-Anthropic fallback. Test tool-call fallback paths explicitly.
store the fallback chain in environment variables or a config file so you can update it without redeployment.
Set different timeout budgets per tier:
the primary can wait longer (it is worth it); fallbacks should have tighter deadlines to avoid compounding latency.
track model_used, fallback_reason, latency_ms, and tokens_used for every LLM call. Route these to your observability platform.
Test the fallback path in CI:
use dependency injection or a mock that randomly raises RateLimitError to exercise the fallback logic in automated tests.
if downstream code parses structured output, run the parser against the fallback response before returning it.
users and stakeholders should know that responses from Tier 2 and below may differ in quality and depth.
Prefer circuit breakers over linear retries:
a circuit breaker that opens after 5 failures saves all subsequent calls from full timeout waits during an outage.
in the retry cycle you advance to the next model. The worst case is: (primary_timeout) + (fallback1_timeout) + ... The best case is immediate fallback when the circuit is already open.

(also called hedging): fire the primary and the first fallback simultaneously after a short delay (e.g., 200 ms). Cancel the slower one when either responds. This caps worst-case latency at primary_timeout + 200 ms instead of 2× primary_timeout. The cost is slightly higher token usage when both models respond near-simultaneously.
At scale, per-model failure counters should live in a
(Redis, Memcached) so that all agent worker processes see the same circuit state. A circuit opened by one worker should immediately protect all workers from wasting time on the same failed model.

as a percentage of total calls. A fallback rate above 5% on the primary suggests the primary is undersized or the rate limit quota is too low—address the root cause rather than relying permanently on fallbacks.
Drop-in replacement for the OpenAI SDK; supports 100+ models; configure fallbacks in YAML
ChatOpenAI.with_fallbacks() wraps any Runnable with a fallback chain
Provides @retry decorator with backoff; pair with manual model rotation
Store failure counts with TTL; redis-py is the standard Python client
Implements Fowler's circuit breaker pattern; works with any callable
Raises typed exceptions (RateLimitError, APIStatusError) that map cleanly to fallback triggers
Track model_calls_total{model, status} to monitor fallback rates in real time
Route by task type, not failure
each provider in the fallback chain needs its own API key stored as a separate secret. Never share keys across providers.
cross-provider fallbacks may send data to a different jurisdiction. Verify that all fallback providers meet your data residency and compliance requirements before deploying.
different models have different safety filtering. A prompt blocked by the primary may pass through a fallback model's filters. This is a security surface, not a feature.
log which model served each request. For regulated industries, you may need to demonstrate that a specific approved model handled sensitive data.
if all agents share one API key, a spike in one agent's traffic can trigger rate limiting affecting all others. Use separate keys or per-agent quotas at scale.

ensure the emergency fallback is always available. If the last model in the chain is itself an external API, a full cloud outage leaves you with nothing. A locally hosted model or rule-based response is the true last resort.
A fallback chain maps model priority to availability:
the primary serves normal traffic; fallbacks serve degraded traffic without surfacing errors to users.
once a model's circuit is OPEN, subsequent calls skip it immediately rather than waiting out a full timeout on every attempt.
Context-window mismatches are a common source of fallback failures:
always truncate or summarise history before re-sending to a model with a smaller window.
fallback is invisible by design; without instrumentation, prolonged primary outages will go unnoticed.
Parallel hedging is the low-latency variant:
fire primary and fallback simultaneously after a short delay and cancel the loser—best for latency-critical paths.
inject artificial errors in staging to verify that every tier in the chain produces acceptable outputs.
Proactive routing and reactive fallback are complementary:
route cheap tasks to cheap models by default, and use fallback for when the designated model is unavailable.
